title: Publishing container ports as UNIX sockets
body:
### Feature request description

Support exposing/publishing container's internal TCP ports as a UNIX socket on the host. To be clear, I'm not taliing about Podman's control UNIX socket, but about the actual port that the service running inside a container will expose. This would allow easier access control to services and reverse proxy setup.

### Suggest potential solution

There should be an option to bind a container port to the UNIX socket on the host. For example, with Quadlets, it could be done like:

```systemd
PublishPort=unix//path/to/socket.sock|0200:5050
```

This would create a UNIX socket file on the host at `/path/to/socket.sock`. That socket would then proxy its traffic to the port TCP 5050 inside the container, where the actual containerized service would be listening. Then, a reverse proxy could just be configured to proxy a matching traffic to the correct UNIX socket.

I'm not sure about the exact syntax that should be used, this one was inspired by [Caddy's network addresses](https://caddyserver.com/docs/conventions#network-addresses) syntax. It should support specifying a host path to the UNIX socket, its permissions, maybe also user/group if possible, and whether it should be TCP or UDP port.

Similarly, it should be possible to set this by running Podman directly or using Podman/Docker Compose YAML files.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

One option for configuring reverse proxy is of course to use a normal TCP port. However, this has a disadventage that it requires all containers to publish their ports to localhost. This means that any program running on the host (or another container) will be access all other services directly, which can be problematic if the reverse proxy is configured to do access control. When containers are running rootless, it also requires them to be using unprivileged ports, and there is no access control for which containers can bind to specific ports (maybe it's possible to configure something in iptables/nftables, but that would be quite complex). Additionally, since ports can only be numbers, managing this is harder as you need to remeber the specific ports of all services and make sure there are no conflicts.

Another option could be to use a shared network between the reverse proxy and all services, so it would be able to directly access them. However, this still allows services to communicate directly with each other (without an additional firewall configuration), requires the reverse proxy to be also containerized, and doesn't work when services are running under different Linux users.

Because of this, UNIX sockets seem like a good solution, as you can fine-grade access using normal file permissions and make sure a specific socket can only be accessed by the reverse proxy and a target service.

However, many programs do not support listening on UNIX sockets directly, and can only listen on a TCP/UDP port inside a container.

One solution is then to run a sidecar container on the same network with something like `socat` that proxies the UNIX socket (which is mounted as a volume) to the TCP port:

```
socat unix-listen:/data/service.sock,umask=007,fork,reuseaddr tcp-connect:container:80
```

However, this requires running an additional container just to proxy connections, and I've heard that `socat` doesn't have great performance as it needs to fork for each connection.

Similarly, you could run Nginx or HAProxy instead of `socat`, which probably scale better, but this is still an additional service that needs to be running and configured, and has an additional overhead.

I have found [`systemd-socket-proxyd`](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/systemd-socket-proxyd.html), which works similarly as `socat`, but has better performance as it uses event-driven design. However, because it uses socket activation, it needs to be configured with `.service` and `.socket` files on the host, and because it's running directly on the host, it cannot access the port inside a container that isn't exposed/published.

I've tried something by running `systemd-socket-proxyd` using `podman unshare`, which would allow it to run inside the container's network namespace and access its ports, but it seems this breaks some file descriptors so socket activation doesn't work anymore.

So, if having a built-in option to expose ports as UNIX sockets would be unfeasable, at least having some simple way to run `systemd-socket-proxyd` on the host in a way that it could access unpublished ports without too much config would probably also be fine.

### Additional context

I'm using Caddy as a reverse proxy and then have various services running inside containers. Because services may be managed by different developers, each of them is running under own Linux user using rootless Podman (along with their databases, Redis, etc.). Also because of this, I don't want to expose each service directly to localhost, as this could cause conflicts betweeen services, but instead use UNIX sockets with specific file permissions.

Basically, I wanted that each service exposes a UNIX socket to `/var/lib/caddy/service/service.sock`, where permissions are configured so that only Caddy and the service can access that directory. Caddy would then be configured to proxy the connector to the correct socket.

comments.body:
My general answer would be no. I don't really want to invent yet another way on how to deal with port forwarding, we already have way to many things to take care of there.
At the minimum as root port forwarding is done by firewall rules on the host without a running process around so there is no way to do that without major changes.

For rootless we have pasta as process so it could technically act as forwarder to do that. Pasta specific features are exposed as cli option in the network options so this would be usable from podman if pasta exposes a way to do that.

But overall I would not be interested in making this a generic podman feature.

> I have found [systemd-socket-proxyd](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/systemd-socket-proxyd.html), which works similarly as socat, but has better performance as it uses event-driven design. However, because it uses socket activation, it needs to be configured with .service and .socket files on the host, and because it's running directly on the host, it cannot access the port inside a container that isn't exposed/published.

I would say that is general the recommendation use systemd socat activation which is more efficient as you do not need to proxy anything. As for no access to the port inside the container well nothing prevents your service to listen both on the passed in unix socket fd and its created tcp socket at the same time.

> For rootless we have pasta as process so it could technically act as forwarder to do that.

Yeah, it could probably be done in pasta. But wouldn't podman still have to implement support for this?

> I would say that is general the recommendation use systemd socat activation which is more efficient as you do not need to proxy anything.

The main use-case for this feature is for services that do not support listening on UNIX sockets and do not support socket activation, which is, at least from my experience, quite a lot of services.

Is it possible to still configure it for such services? Or is there another way to do this?

> As for no access to the port inside the container well nothing prevents your service to listen both on the passed in unix socket fd and its created tcp socket at the same time.

Well, sure, but in this case, it's the flaw in the service and it's kinda the service's fault if it gets exposed. The idea is that the service has the possibility to expose its socket in a way that only specific programs (reverse proxy) can access it.

> For rootless we have pasta as process so it could technically act as forwarder to do that. Pasta specific features are exposed as cli option in the network options so this would be usable from podman if pasta exposes a way to do that.

See also: https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=200.

> But wouldn't podman still have to implement support for this?

It would be relatively straightforward at that point, and you could still pass options transparently before Podman adds support for it (`--net pasta:--option,value...`).

Until this is implemented in pasta, is there any workaround how I could achieve this? As mentioned above, I haven't managed to set up systemd-socket-proxyd, and many services don't support socket activation, so I also can't use that.

> Until this is implemented in pasta, is there any workaround how I could achieve this?

Two ideas come to my mind:
- contribute to the implementation in pasta (for example, implement an "address table" in parallel to the forwarding rules table, or, simpler starting point, implement the specific UNIX domain socket handling, analogous to TCP and UDP listening sockets)
- use socat for the moment being. You could run several instances on the host. I don't really expect any performance drawback from doing that

Just commenting on this. I have many services where i run --publish and then `socat` as the standard way to get published services into a unix sockets.
I came to this issue because an llm told me I can bypass this using something like `-p unix:///my.sock:80`, mentioning this issue as source. Would be great to have this feature!

I have no interest supporting podman specific syntax for just one port forwarder, if we do such things they must work for all port forwards and at the time I do not see this happening as such I am going to close this. The pasta feature is already tracked there and if implemented could be used regardless of podman support.
